data.title: Redwood Trust: The 9.125% Baby Bonds Are Attractive Given The Financials
data.description:
Thesis

Redwood Trust (RWT) is an mREIT that operates in the mortgage banking business and also runs an investment portfolio sourced via its origination platforms. We have covered liabilities (i.e., bonds) from this company before, and today we are going to have a look at a series of very interesting baby bonds that are set to have their first call date in the next 30 days.

Latest company financials

Redwood reported results that slightly exceeded expectations, with the common equity rallying hard on the news. Let us have a look at the drivers behind the performance:

Over 80% of the firm's capital is allocated to two core segments:

  • Investments
  • Mortgage Banking

The company managed double-digit return on capital ('ROC') on both of these segments, with the mortgage banking business an outperformer at a 29% ROC figure. Mortgage banking is a business that is very sensitive to lower mortgage rates; thus, the recent decrease in interest rates has helped. Lower rates mean lower monthly payments for the same loan amount, thus allowing more people to qualify for mortgages:

The company's volumes are up year on year, thus enhancing profitability and quality of business. As per the earnings call, management expects further volumes if mortgage rates continue to compress:

With our network, products and distribution, we are well positioned to benefit from a broader refinance wave should mortgage rates fall below 6%.

The company runs an 'originate to distribute' mortgage banking model, which complements a buy-to-hold investment portfolio, thus creating two distinct earnings engines. We can see the company shifting more capital towards this business segment as rates are moving lower:

While in fiscal year 2024 the company allocated 47% in capital to mortgage banking, that has shifted to 62% in 2025 and is growing. This is a smart move since ROC metrics justify such a shift.

The investments segment is also posting robust results, which could further improve as rates move lower and funding costs are reduced:

Do note that this segment consists of 'in-house' originated securities via the various company channels (i.e., Sequoia Securities, etc.). As the portfolio cost of funds has come down, the profitability has increased as well. Expect more of this as Fed Funds and SOFR move lower, thus decreasing the cost of short-term funding. The company's funding profile shows how 47% of the debt becomes callable in 2026, thus giving them flexibility in terms of refinancing.

When summarized in the income statement, we can clearly see the profitability drivers:

Mortgage banking net interest is higher while also contributing to non-interest income as well. Similarly, the net interest income for the investments segment is higher, thus pushing the company to a net profitability figure from a small loss last quarter.

While the common equity rallied in excess of +20% on the back of these encouraging results, do note it is still trading below book value, which was $7.36 per share as of Q4 2025.

The company does run leverage

Despite the very healthy earnings, investors should not forget Redwood runs significant leverage:

The company runs a 4.6x recourse leverage ratio, up from 4x the quarter before. While we agree with what has been done as of late - increase leverage for those sectors providing for a high ROC and positive net income—gearing runs both ways. A recessionary environment can work against the company here if mortgage demand was to falter and income were to shrink.

Management also noted that while leverage is higher than in previous years, they are turning over loans much faster, thus being more comfortable with more debt on the balance sheet to fuel the mortgage banking segment (which is highly profitable).

Debentures details

The 9.125% Senior Notes due 2029 (RWTN) were issued back in January 2024 and have their first optional call date coming up in March 2026. A healthy financial performance by Redwood means that they can refinance these bonds later this year at a lower rate (if they continue to post two more quarters of good financials). In turn, that means RWTN are not long-dated risky bonds but high-paying, low-maturity securities. The full details are

  • Coupon: 9.125%
  • Maturity date: March 1, 2029
  • Call date: March 1, 2026
  • Price: $25.1
  • Par: $25/share

When companies establish good track records with positive financials, they can access debt markets at cheaper rates. It is the case for Redwood as well, which could see its required market coupons shrink if it continues to post positive net income figures. Furthermore, given the price reduction in rates by the Fed, we could also see all-in yields decrease, which would help further.

This simply means the company might be able to refinance RWTN much cheaper later this year, thus making RWTN an attractive short-term play.

We can clearly see the capital markets pricing an improved picture on the preferred equity side: the 10% preferred shares (RWT.PR.A) are now trading at par, thus yielding 10%. Preferred shares are subordinated to bonds in a restructuring, thus usually trading much wider. The closing of the gap between the current yield on RWT.PR.A and RWTN tell us that equity markets now see RWT very favorably.

At this macro juncture, we favor RWTN over RWT.PR.A, with the preferred shares priced for perfection. Even if we do get a mild recession, but RWT's financials improve, we are going to see the company refinance RWTN, thus making the securities an attractive short term play with a yield above 9%.

Conclusion

RWTN are baby bonds from Redwood. The company has posted very robust financials in Q4 2025, finally returning to profitability. The trend is set to continue with lower mortgage rates and Fed cuts this year. RWTN was issued with a coupon above 9%, making it an expensive piece of debt. If the company continues its profitability trajectory, the company will refinance the bonds in 2026, thus making them an attractive short-dated play.

data.title: Gold Breaches $5000/Oz: Has The Bullish Trajectory Resumed?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The price of gold has breached the $5000/oz handle once more. A surprise, given the overwhelming consensus by analysts that the recent rally had shown signs of being a 'dead cat bounce,' given the grind we saw from gold prices.

However, gold has once again grinded its way back above the key $5000/oz handle, with more questions now being asked. Chief among them is whether the precious metal will be able to kick in from here or will we be in for more indecision for the rest of the week?

What was behind the recent pullback in gold prices?

There have been a lot of mixed messages as to what the reasons were behind gold's recent slide. In my view, the initial pullback in gold prices was driven by a strengthening US dollar and a shift toward risk-off sentiment across global markets.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart, February 18, 2026

These price swings were further intensified by thin liquidity, as major Asian markets were closed for the Lunar New Year, making gold more vulnerable to macroeconomic shifts and currency fluctuations.

Despite this sensitivity to the dollar and broader market nerves, the recent decline appears to be a temporary correction rather than a long-term trend. As Asian markets reopen and liquidity returns to normal levels, gold appears to have found firmer support.

Given the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty and solid underlying fundamentals, any further price dips will likely attract fresh buying interest from investors looking for a safe haven.

Looking Ahead: US data, US dollar dynamics and geopolitical risks

Looking ahead, US markets will focus on housing data, remarks from Fed officials, GDP figures for Q4 2025, and the release of the Fed's preferred inflation measure, the core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Price Index.

All of these will have some role to play in gold's next move as markets remain somewhat cautious given the uncertainties at play. Looking at the fear and greed index and markets are still in fear territory with a score of 43. This is also keeping gold prices supported, as fear tends to keep market participants interested in safe-haven bets.

Technical Outlook - Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical standpoint, gold has resumed its long-term bullish trajectory, but significant challenges remain. The overall long-term structure remains bullish (upward), but the shorter-term chart (H4) shows a neutral-to-bearish tilt as the market consolidates its recent 15% drop from the $5,500 highs.

Looking at the four-hour chart below, the range between 5096 and 4760 will be key in the near term. Looking at the potential for an upside breakout and the swing high at 5046 (printed on February 13) is the first point of contention.

A four-hour candle close above this level may open the door for a range break beyond the 5096 mark and open up a potential 330 odd dollar move to the upside.

Further supporting the bullish breakout narrative is the period-14 RSI on the H4 chart, which is now above the 50 level, hinting at bullish momentum.

Conversely, a move lower here first needs to navigate support at 4908 before the swing low around the 4860 handle comes into focus. Only then will the range low around 4760 become an area of focus.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour Chart, February 18, 2026

Original Post

data.title: Bombardier: A Buy As The Free Cash Flow Flywheel Accelerates
data.description:
In January, I downgraded Bombardier (BDRAF, BDRBF) stock from strong buy to buy as strong share price appreciation left little fundamental upside while Bombardier got caught up in a rift between the US and Canada over the certification of Gulfstream business jets. Since then, Bombardier shares have lost 9% of their value. The company recently provided its full year and outlook for 2026, which provides a good moment to revisit Bombardier’s investment case.

Bombardier Sales and Margins Expand In 2025 On Successful Turnaround

Bombardier's earnings presentation shows that 2025 revenues grew 10.2% to $9.55 billion. Manufacturing revenues rose 9% to $7.25 billion, driven by a 7.5% increase in airplane deliveries, with the remainder being driven by the delivery mix leaning more towards large-cabin business jets. Services sales increased 13% to $2.3 billion as Bombardier’s installed base keeps growing and the global services footprint is increasing. Services now account for 24% of sales, providing a strong recurring revenue stream. We also note that the company had $1 billion in defense-related sales, which the company sees as a growth opportunity in the years ahead.

Adjusted EBITDA grew almost 15% to $1.56 billion. Margins expanded 60 basis points to 16.3%. The margins were driven by defense sales, which come at a higher margin; the higher overall delivery profile driving operating leverage; and the mix leaning towards bigger business jets, which come at better margins. Services sales and sales growth also provide strong structural margin support. We note that supply chain issues brought a 150 basis point headwind. So, absent supply chain challenges, the margins would have looked even more impressive. Free cash flow increased substantially from $232 million to $1.07 billion, driven by lower capital expenditures and customer advances.

The Growth Drivers for Bombardier

Bombardier has several growth drivers. Demand supports higher production rates, but we note that growth is constrained by supply chain challenges that provide a margin headwind. So, that is a current drag that should start dissolving in the second half of 2026. Essentially, that is a near-term risk that should result in a tailwind in the second half of 2026 and into 2027, driving lower working capital requirements for de-risking inventory and higher production. The growing services footprint also drives revenue opportunities for Bombardier, while defense is a major growth opportunity as well. Defense sales are not going to be adding billions of dollars to sales and may be somewhat volatile as volumes are not high. However, supported by higher defense budgets, we could see volume growth with even better margins for defense products.

The operational turnaround for Bombardier is now largely complete, having completed the certification of its new jets, transitioned manufacturing to a modern assembly facility, and rolled out a services footprint to better capitalize on aftermarket sales opportunities. The company has also substantially retired debt. There still is legacy debt at high-interest rates in the books, and the final step is to partially retire that debt with the remainder being refinanced against more attractive terms due to lower interest rates and a better financial framework for Bombardier.

Bombardier Guidance Is Not Inspiring, But Perfectly Understandable

For 2026, Bombardier is targeting more than 157 deliveries with revenues of more than $10 billion, marking 4.7% growth as the minimum. Adjusted EBITDA is set at $1.625 billion as a minimum, indicating stable margins. Free cash flow is expected to be between $600 million and $1 billion. Bombardier has a significant opportunity to improve cash flow conversion as it still paid $418 million (Annual Report, Page 42) in interest expenses in 2025. As the company retires more debt or refinances debt at lower interest rates, it has a substantial opportunity to increase free cash flow as its capital structure improves. I consider this one of Bombardier’s hidden free cash flow flywheels.

The guidance itself is not an inspiring one, but it is not indicative of upside being limited by capacity or demand. Bombardier is guiding the way it did because supply chain issues, primarily on business jet engines, are still keeping a damper on working capital efficiency and output.

Bombardier Valuation Case Is Complex

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The valuation case for Bombardier is a complex one. The company has traded at a median EV/EBITDA of 10.8x over the past three years. That is substantially below the peer group. Since there are no pure-play business jet manufacturers, we also cannot compile a credible selection of subgroups in the aerospace and defense industry. We do see that in 2025, the company ended the year trading at around 13.2x EV/EBITDA.

Keeping that valuation level steady, which I believe is not unreasonable given that this multiple is also substantially below the peer group multiple of >22x, there would be 8% upside for 2026, and valuing the stock one year ahead of earnings, there would be 21% upside. At a 10.8x multiple, the upside towards 2028 would be 8%. So, that 8% upside is anchored on multiple timeframes and valuation multiples. That would also be my base case price target, and I believe that an optimistic but reasonable price target would be $214.87, indicating 21% upside.

So, for Bombardier, the coming twelve months will not only be of importance to show it can deliver on its outlook for 2026 but also for the market to set a valuation multiple that increasingly starts reflecting the market appreciation for Bombardier as a company that has largely recovered from its past financial performance.

For Bombardier, we note that annual sales growth of 4.2% annually is not impressive. At the same time, continued improvement in operating leverage and margin mix between large and small cabin sales, as well as manufacturing and services sales, should drive margins higher. Cash conversion in 2025 was strong, and for now it is set to dip and gradually improve as more debt with associated interest costs is being removed, and operating leverage should drive cash performance. Potentially, there is some upside to the cash flow performance on order inflow, as business jets have an advance payment profile or pre-delivery payment profile that is more frontloaded compared to commercial airplanes. Order intake could positively enhance the overall cash conversion ratios.

We also note that even with accelerating debt repayments, the company should be able to see its cash balance substantially increase, while the net debt leverage by 2028 could be close to zero. So, Bombardier has substantial space to consider share repurchases in the years ahead and most definitely from 2029 onward.

Conclusion: Bombardier Turnaround Is Largely Complete, The Real Growth Starts Now

Bombardier’s turnaround is now largely complete, with the business positioned for large-cabin business jet production and a bigger services footprint. The only overhang we still see is the debt load, which is being managed proactively to reduce interest costs and supplier bottlenecks. Other than that, demand is strong enough for Bombardier to substantially grow its output from H2 2026 and beyond.

So, from an operational perspective, I remain bullish on Bombardier, but it should be noted that it is somewhat unknown where the marketplaces Bombardier in terms of the valuation multiples applied. I believe that at this stage a 13.5x multiple growing to 15x would be very reasonable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Incyte Remains Undervalued As Opzelura And Niktimvo Scale
data.description:
Since my last article in August, "Incyte: Wall Street Underestimates Strength of Ruxolitinib Portfolio," Incyte shares (INCY) are up 19.1%.

Thus, it outperformed the S&P 500 [6.8% return] (SPY), as well as the so-called 'bellwether' for the biotech investors, the VanEck Biotech ETF [19.3%] (BBH).

And I ask myself: What has aroused investors' interest in it? Maybe solid sales of Minjuvi/ Monjuvi, Opzelura or Incyte's R&D achievements?

On December 22 last year, Japan's MHLW approved Zynyz with chemotherapy medications, carboplatin and paclitaxel, for 1L treatment of advanced anal cancer.

Also, in my opinion, Wall Street sees that under CEO Bill Meury, Incyte's EBITDA is becoming less dependent on its flagship cancer drug called Jakafi, whose U.S. exclusivity ends in 2028.

In turn, its sales grew 7.1% year-over-year to $828.2 million in Q4, beating even my 'best case' scenario by $15 million.

And in this quarter, Jakafi's share of Incyte's total revenue dropped sharply to 55% from about 65%, the level around which it had fluctuated from Q3 2024 to Q1 2025.

I believe this shift happened because of the launch of Niktimvo on August 14 last year for the treatment of GVHD and label expansion for Opzelura.

While Zynyz and Minjuvi/ Monjuvi generated $73.6 million in net revenues for Incyte in Q4, up 115.2% year-on-year , they remain in the shadow of its other drugs.

I'm now talking about Opzelura and Niktimvo, because of which, as well as other factors included in my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, I continue to cover Incyte with a 'Buy' rating.

Opzelura continues to strengthen Incyte's ruxolitinib franchise

First, let me remind you that Opzelura [ruxolitinib] is a cream formulation of ruxolitinib.

On July 18, 2022, this JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor became the first FDA-approved agent for the treatment of skin pigment disorder called NSV (nonsegmental vitiligo).

In Q4, its sales grew 28.3% year-over-year and 10.3% quarter-over-quarter to $207.3 million.

I estimate that the increase in demand for Opzelura partly reflects its status as the 'standard of care' for the treatment of NSV, as well as its label expansion on September 18 last year.

It is now approved in the US for 3 indications.

And also, CEO Bill Meury was quite confident about Opzelura's prospects.

The pediatric launch for Opzelura AD is off to a strong start in the United States, with sales already annualizing around $30 million.

Both dermatologists and parents are increasingly seeking nonsteroidal options for atopic dermatitis, driven by concerns about long-term steroid use. International sales for Opzelura in vitiligo doubled to $130 million in 2025.

In '26, we expect sales of $750 million to $790 million, representing roughly a 15% increase at the midpoint. This estimate is based primarily on continued double-digit volume growth in the United States for AD and vitiligo, partially offset by price actions to expand formulary coverage as well as sustained double-digit growth internationally off of a larger base as we lap the strong full year launch for vitiligo in Europe.

Most of the benefits of the moderate AD launch in Europe in the second half of this year are expected in '27 and beyond.

However, Big Pharma also wants a piece of the vitiligo treatment market, estimated at $2.54 billion by 2034.

So, as I said in my last article, "AbbVie: Dominating Immunology While Building Oncology Upside", on February 3, AbbVie (ABBV) submitted applications to the FDA/EMA to expand the label of Rinvoq for the treatment of non-segmental vitiligo.

This means that Rinvoq (upadacitinib 15 mg) could become the first competitor to Opzelura.

And now I want to understand if Incyte will remain the market leader.

To do this, I analyzed the 52-week results from the Phase 3 TRuE-V1 and TRuE-V2 trials (NCT04052425, NCT04057573).

At week 52 from the start of treatment, about 50% and 75% of patients taking Opzelura achieved improvement in F-VASI75 and F-VASI50, respectively.

For comparison, in AbbVie's Viti-Up studies (NCT06118411), at week 48, 25.2% and 48.1% of participants in the upadacitinib group achieved F-VASI 75 and F-VASI 50, respectively.

As a result, my first interim conclusion is that in an indirect comparison, Opzelura was shown to be superior to Rinvoq. And as I noted in the table above, I like Incyte's cream because it acts locally, rather than as AbbVie's once-daily pill, which is a systemic treatment.

Thus, I believe that the likelihood of depigmentation after discontinuing Rinvoq is higher than with Opzelura.

Niktimvo begins to dominate the chronic GvHD treatment market

Niktimvo is another drug that should offset the loss of Jakafi's exclusivity in 2028.

But, in my view, Niktimvo (axatilimab) remains under the radar of Wall Street.

This CSF-1R-blocking antibody is approved for the treatment of some patients with cGvHD (chronic graft-versus-host disease).

Simply put, cGvHD is a serious complication in which the donor's T cells, following a bone marrow/stem cell transplant, view the recipient's tissues as foreign and attack them.

By the way, it is a common cause of late morbidity and death.

For the three months ended December 31, Niktimvo sales were $56 million, up 22.3% QoQ, and just as importantly, beating my 'best case' scenario by $3.1 million due to its deeper utilization in BMT centers.

In a call, Incyte's Head R&D Pablo Cagnoni indicated that the results of the studies evaluating axatilimab as a "1L chronic GVHD" would be known in 2027-2028.

In GVHD, we're advancing Niktimvo in 2 first-line studies evaluating it in combination with ruxolitinib as well as in combination with steroids. Data from these trials are expected in early 2027 and early 2028, respectively.

But to my regret, nothing else interesting was said about it.

Risks

In the table below, I've highlighted several risks.

However, the key ones are weak sales of Tabrecta [$7.1 million in Q4 2025, compared with $6.4 million a year earlier], competition from J&J's Darzalex/Darzalex Faspro (JNJ) and Legend Biotech's Carvykti (LEGN), as well as a 6% QoQ decline in Incyte's EBIT margin to 25.6%.

Takeaway

So Incyte posted another strong quarter.

Its revenue rose 27.8% year-over-year to $1.51 billion and beat my 'base case' scenario by $80 million.

Meanwhile, Incyte's non-GAAP EPS was $1.8 compared to $1.43 for the same period in 2024. It fell short of Wall Street expectations, in my view, due to the ongoing extensive Phase III trial programs for Niktimvo, ruxolitinib, and INCB123667.

But again, despite this slight miss, retail investors' pessimism and skepticism about Incyte are fading.

I believe one of the reasons INCY has become a 'favorite' is that its non-GAAP P/E ratio will drop from 14.89x in 2025 to 10.5x in 2029, which is below its 5-year average by 35.5% and 54.5%, respectively.

Also, given Incyte's 65.9% year-over-year growth of total cash and short-term investments to $3.58 billion, I continue to see it as an attractive target for long-term investors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Pressures Rebuild For Long Dates
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist and Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

Time to move back up for 10Y euro rates

The equity jitters from the US are starting to ease, which brings significant upside potential for 10Y euro rates in the near term. A worsening of risk sentiment on the back of AI concerns helped push the 10Y swap rate some 20bp below the peak in January.

But Wednesday saw better stock performance, and the VIX, as a measure of risk sentiment, eased significantly lower. UST yields made a move higher, helped partly by better macro data, but somehow Bund yields are still stuck too low in our view.

We’re looking at a bear steepening from here, as the front end of the EUR curve remains firmly anchored. Markets price in a probability of around 30% for another ECB cut this year, which seems fair given the eurozone’s growth recovery is still fragile.

To move this, we would likely need a sharp fall in inflation, a much stronger euro, or a series of deteriorating growth figures. We don’t see any of these factors materialising in the very near term. Meanwhile, the 10Y rate has room to rise, especially if US Treasury yields back up again.

With equity jitters easing, the level of Bund yields seems too low

Foreigners sold US Treasuries in December

Latest US Treasury International Capital (TIC) system data show that foreigners sold a net US$88bn in December. There was a wide spread of net sellers, with the UK, Japan and Norway the largest.

Through 2025 as a whole, there was net buying of US$652bn, with the largest buying from the UK, Japan, Belgium (custodial) and Canada. Overall, foreign buying of Treasuries remains positive in net terms. At the same time, it's been clear that the biggest of the buying is among domestics, as they were net buyers to the tune of US$1.6tr through 2025.

Foreigners own about a third of the Treasury debt currently, but domestics are dominating the net buying, with the Federal Reserve a net seller. That said, the Fed in fact bought a net US$97bn in December, as it resumed its bill-buying programme, and also bought some short-dated bonds.

Overall, these data are not a dominating influence in setting where Treasury rates sit, as any downsizing in net foreign buying is being broadly offset by domestic buying, and it does not appear that there is a material price concession attached as a result of these dynamics.

Meanwhile, the latest 20yr auction tailed on Wednesday. It was not pretty as the tail was 2bp – effectively a concession versus the secondary market. The indirect bid (typically includes central banks) was weaker than usual. While the auction was not great, it was also the 20yr, which has a bit of a reputation as not being the market's favourite line.

Thursday’s events and market view

In terms of data, the eurozone will release preliminary consumer confidence data for February. ECB officials scheduled to speak on Thursday are Cipollone and de Guindos.

The US releases the December trade balance, pending home sales as well as import/export prices, though the main focus will likely remain the state of the jobs market, with the initial claims offering a more timely insight. The Fed’s Bostic, Bowman and Kashkari will be speaking.

France and Spain will be active in primary markets. France will auction a new 3Y bond alongside the reopening of two intermediate maturity lines and three inflation-linked bonds. Overall, France aims to raise €15bn in the auctions. Spain sells up to €5.5bn across 3Y, 5Y and 10Y bond lines.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sixth Street Specialty Lending: The 9.6% Dividend Yield Could Be Attractive Against Low Nonaccruals Rate
data.description:
Sixth Street Specialty Lending's (TSLX) sustained dip since September has come on the back of total and net investment income that is seeing weakness in response to recent Fed rate cuts, aggregated with market fears around the future of software-backed credit in the shadow of AI. The BDC last declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.46 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.84 per share annualized for a 9.60% dividend yield. There was also a $0.01 per share supplemental dividend, a slight dip from the prior supplemental distribution of $0.03 per share, and a distribution where shareholders gain an incremental 0.21% yield this year.

TSLX is externally managed by an affiliate of San Francisco-based Sixth Street Partners, a global investment firm with $125 billion in assets under management. Whether TSLX is a buy after its material retracement from its 52-week high should be based on the ongoing strength of coverage of the base dividend, cadence with originations against pressure on credit yields from Fed rate cuts, and the BDC's ability to maintain strong credit quality while managing its balance sheet for growth.

The dividend yield has moved to its highest level in 5 years, with this representing the intersection of perfect headwinds that have hit investor sentiment towards private credit. The September collapse of First Brands Group and Tricolor sparked fears around "credit cockroaches", 75 basis points of rate cuts in the second half of 2025, and an AI-driven selloff in software-linked securities. TSLX generated fiscal 2025 fourth-quarter total investment income of $108.25 million, a dip of 12.5% over its year-ago comp and sequentially from $109.44 million in the third quarter. This dip was led by pressure on TSLX's net interest margin, with net investment income ("NII") coming in at $0.53 per share during the fourth quarter. I last covered TSLX in October with a Hold rating, with the investment outlook for BDCs having dipped since then.

Dividend Coverage, Originations, and Credit Quality

TSLX's NII per share meant 115% coverage for the base dividend yield. This positive depth of coverage does provide a significant hedge against the risk of a dividend cut, just as the list of BDCs cutting their dividend continues to grow. CION Investment Corporation (CION), BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC), and Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) have all recently cut their base dividend distributions. This environment demands that BDC investors be materially more selective about the securities they choose to allocate their capital. The CME FedWatch Tool currently has 50 basis points of rate cuts through 2026 as its base case at a probability of 30.7%. TSLX invests in loans to US-based middle-market companies, with $3.35 billion in investment at fair value as of the end of its fourth quarter and with total assets at $3.42 billion.

Total assets were down from $3.58 billion at the start of the year as repayments continue to run ahead of originations. The portfolio came with an 89.2% first-lien debt allocation and a 96.3% floating-rate debt position. Investment commitments came in at $1.08 billion through 2025, with investments sold and repaid coming in at $1.20 billion. Hence, net funded investment activity was negative at $302 million for the year. This represented a pullback in origination cadence versus prior years. Total nonaccruals remained unchanged sequentially at 0.6% at fair value as of the end of the fourth quarter.

TSLX is currently swapping hands for $19.23 per share, a 13.25% premium to net asset value ('NAV') per share of $16.98 as of the end of the fourth quarter. This dipped by $0.16 per share sequentially, but with the commons still trading at a premium, TSLX is in a position where it can issue new shares from an accretive baseline to diversify its capital stack. The BDC is diversified across a range of industries, with Internet Industries its largest allocation at 18.3% of its portfolio.

Conclusion

The recent market panic around the potential of AI to heavily disrupt the business models of software companies is likely to become an investment story that will play out through most of this year. For BDCs, bears consider credit originated to software companies as likely to see a material non-accrual rate in the years ahead, as a possible mass-commoditization of software from AI plays out. However, the jury is still out on the extent and pace at which vibe coding will actually erode the structural moats built by software companies. These moats range from client relationships, sales teams to enterprise-level SLAs. TSLX's allocation to software is also more nuanced than its peers. Hence, I currently view the panic around software-linked credit as overblown. TSLX is seeing yield pressure, pushing down NII and its dividend coverage, which does necessitate a Hold rating.

data.title: New Gold And Coeur Mining: Bigger Might Really Be Better
data.description:
New Gold & Coeur Mining Merger

Coeur Mining’s (CDE) $7 billion acquisition of New Gold (NGD) creates a top-10 precious metals company and top-5 silver producer globally. The combined entity, upon closing, will produce an estimated 900K oz gold, ~20M oz silver, and ~100M lbs copper in 2026, making the miner a truly diversified metals producer with leverage to not just one, but all three commodities.

When Coeur Mining announced its $7 billion all-stock acquisition of New Gold back in November, I have to admit, my first reaction was mixed. In my 15+ years investing in this sector, I've learned the hard way that mega-mergers in mining don’t always work out. See the Kinross/Red Back Mining debacle back in 2010 as a prime example - $6 billion in write-downs for Kinross - although I like to think that miners have largely learned from these expensive past mistakes.

But the more I dig into this latest deal, the more I like it. I don't view this as Coeur overpaying for a mediocre asset or company. This is two mid-tier producers combining into something very valuable and genuinely hard to replicate: a high-quality, 100% North American senior miner with seven operating mines, strong and growing free cash flow, and a deep growth pipeline. And that's the type of company that commands a premium valuation.

New Gold shareholders get 0.4959 Coeur shares per NGD share (roughly 38% of the combined company), and shareholders approved the deal on January 27 (the B.C. Supreme Court signed off on January 30). The only remaining hurdle is the Investment Canada Act, and both companies expect the transaction to close in H1 2026.

I think we'll see a slight re-rating post-closing of the acquisition, so I've initiated New Gold with a BUY rating here, as I feel the combined company will outperform the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) as a benchmark.

A Diversified Metals Producer (Not Just Gold)

Here’s what the new Coeur looks like post-close.

Metric

Combined company (2026E)

Gold Production

900,000 ounces

Silver Production

20 million oz (top 5 globally)

Copper Production

100 million lbs

Gold-Equivalent Production

1.25 million ounces

EBITDA

$3.0 billion

Free Cash Flow

$2.0 billion

Operating Mines

7 (U.S., Canada, Mexico)

Revenue from U.S./Canada

~80%

Source: Based on company estimates in its press release and in public disclosures.

Investors will get meaningful exposure to three metals that each have their own tailwinds:

  • Gold (GLD) is self-explanatory at these prices ($5,000/oz+) with strong central bank demand and investor demand.
  • But silver (SLV), at 30% of total reserves (post-close of the acquisition), gives the combined company serious leverage to what I believe is the most undervalued precious metal out there following its huge, recent run-up and subsequent correction. Industrial demand for solar and electronics is growing and should continue to do so in the years ahead.
  • Don't sleep on the new company's copper output. At 100 million pounds a year, investors get real exposure to what might be the tightest supply-demand story in commodities. Everyone's talking about AI, but not what powers it. A single AI data center consumes three times as much copper as a traditional facility.

AI data centers alone are expected to consume over 700,000 tons of copper by 2026, and that number keeps climbing. Between the AI buildout, the energy transition, and the aging mine supply globally, copper has a long runway.

How It Stacks Up Against Peers

Company

2026E Production

2025 AISC ($/oz)

P/E (2026)

Jurisdictions

Agnico Eagle (AEM)

3.3–3.5M oz

$1,339

15.10

Canada, Australia, Mexico

Gold Fields (GFI)

2.4–2.8M oz

$1,645

10.96

S. Africa, Americas, Australia

Kinross Gold (KGC)

2.0M oz

$1,500

11.78

Americas, W. Africa

Coeur + New Gold

1.25M oz

$1,150*

~9.5

U.S., Canada, Mexico

Equinox Gold (EQX)

920K oz

$1,850

11.73

Canada, U.S., Brazil, Mexico

*This is my estimate of both companies blended AISC, but they are targeting a 20% reduction in AISC. Coeur's P/E estimate is a combo of New Gold and Coeur.

At 1.25 million gold-equivalent ounces, the combined company slots into the gap between established mid-tiers and the seniors.

On costs, New Gold is already one of the lowest-cost producers in the GDX—its 2025 AISC guidance of $1,025–$1,125 per ounce (midpoint ~$1,075) compares favorably to Coeur’s consolidated $1,215/oz in Q3 2025. Blended, that puts the combined entity around $1,150/oz.

Now here’s where it gets interesting: management is targeting a 20% AISC reduction at three of Coeur’s five mines. If they deliver even half of that, you’re looking at a combined AISC pushing toward $950–$1,000/oz, which would put this company among the lowest-cost senior producers in the world.

On a forward P/E basis, I have the combined company at the lowest valuation among its peers in the table above.

And the new company's jurisdictional quality is arguably best-in-class: 80% U.S. and Canadian revenue versus peers with exposure to West Africa, South America, or other higher-risk regions. Therefore, the company's stock should trade at a premium, not a discount, to its peers.

The K-Zone and Other Growth Levers

New Afton’s K-Zone has more than doubled in size—now 600 metres in strike length and 900 metres vertically, with new mineralization found 550 metres east. New Gold bumped the exploration budget to $22 million (63,000 metres of drilling) and is targeting a maiden resource estimate with a feasibility study in 2026.

Critically, K-Zone can leverage existing C-Zone infrastructure—the plant, tailings facility, and crusher are already built. Any future production comes at a fraction of the greenfield cost.

According to New Gold, preliminary technical studies are underway to assess potential mining scenarios for K-Zone, HW Zone, and D-Zone; together, these have the potential to extend New Afton mine life beyond 2031.

Rainy River has brownfield upside too. Underground development rates improved 45% quarter-over-quarter in Q4. Combine that with Coeur’s own exploration pipeline across Idaho, Nevada, and Mexico, and you’ve got multiple organic growth levers within safe jurisdictions.

M&A is Just Getting Started

One thing I keep coming back to: there are 112 publicly traded gold mining companies right now with a company market cap of over $1 trillion.

With gold above $5,000/oz and cash flows at record levels, the big players have the firepower to consolidate. In the past, we've seen Newmont take out Newcrest, Agnico merge with Kirkland Lake, and now Coeur absorb New Gold.

I don't see this trend slowing down, and I actually expect it to accelerate (and the companies being acquired tend to get taken out at premiums). If you own a well-run, safe-jurisdiction producer with real growth, you’re either going to benefit from the re-rate or get a takeout premium.

That's why I'm actively building up positions in names like New Gold that I feel will likely attract M&A interest over the next year or so with gold prices at these levels. My work right now is focused on a list of prime M&A candidates, primarily across the Americas, where jurisdictional quality and asset fundamentals make them natural acquisition targets.

The Short Squeeze Setup

One more thing worth flagging. Per FINRA data, Coeur has about 70 million shares sold short—roughly 11% of the float (YCharts has it slightly below that, at 10.54% of the float).

That’s a meaningful short position for a company about to close a transformational deal.

If the deal clears, the combined entity reports a strong quarter, and gold holds these levels? Those shorts are going to need to be covered. I’m not guaranteeing a squeeze, but the setup is about as good as you’ll find in this sector.

New Gold & Coeur: What Are the Risks?

Investment Canada Act approval is pending. Both companies expect H1 2026, but regulatory timelines can slip. The outside termination date is May 15, with an extension to August 15. If something derails this, NGD gives back its merger premium in a hurry.

We're seeing two companies combine a total of seven mines in three countries. There's always this risk with M&A: management teams need to mesh, systems need to integrate, and the promised AISC reductions need to actually materialize. Mining mergers don’t always deliver on the slide deck.

Gold, silver, and copper are all at or near elevated levels. A broad pullback in metals hits the entire thesis (although that's the case with any precious metals stock). The multi-commodity exposure cuts both ways.

Bottom Line: This Merged Company is a Buy

New Gold at 7.8x forward earnings is cheap. Coeur at just over 10x isn’t expensive, either. The combined company gives you a top-10 precious metals producer trading at a sub-10 forward P/E ratio, a top-5 silver producer, meaningful copper exposure from the AI-driven demand cycle, 80% safe-jurisdiction revenue, organic growth from K-Zone and Rainy River, and $2 billion in expected annual free cash flow.

The deal is essentially done, as shareholders overwhelmingly approved it. The court approved it. We’re waiting on the regulatory stamp, and both sides expect a close in H1 2026.

In a sector with 112 publicly traded names and consolidation accelerating, I think this combined entity is positioned exactly where you want to be. I'm initiating coverage with a Buy rating.

data.title: Ambev: 2026 World Cup And Brazil's Rate Cuts Create A Powerful Recovery Setup
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Ambev (ABEV), I highlighted their strong financial position, compelling valuation, and attractive dividend despite near-term pressure from broader macro factors, reiterating their Buy rating.

Although the stock is up about 20% since covering it a bit over a month ago and 40% since beginning the coverage back in August, I still believe ABEV has some room left to grow, as the company can benefit from a mix of near- and long-term tailwinds as the region’s leader.

Internal Developments

Ambev’s Q4 report was solid overall, with non-GAAP EPS in line with the market’s expectations and a slight beat on revenue, with solid results across their key markets, delivering a 5.6% increase in Organic Normalized EBITDA in FY25 vs FY24 despite a 3.3% drop in volumes.

The company's free cash flow dropped in FY25 to R$19.86 billion (US$3.80 billion) compared to R$21.35 billion (US$4.09 billion) in 2024, mostly as a result of unfavorable working capital changes, but it's still not terrible for a market cap of ~$48.89 billion, placing them at a P/FCF ratio of 12.87 during a relatively weak year.

Alongside a couple of important near-term tailwinds that I'll mention below, Ambev's guidance expects Brazil Beer Cash COGS (cost of goods sold) per hectoliter to grow between 4.5% and 7.5% in 2026, mostly as a result of commodity headwinds stemming from higher aluminum prices and portfolio mix effects, while the company plans to reinvest into organic growth alongside disciplined M&A activity while returning excess cash to shareholders.

Financially, based on ABEV's latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with the current assets almost covering their current liabilities and nearly zero debt, with ~US$3.57 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents available, which adds a lot of flexibility to an already strong industry leader, which can potentially use debt in the future if they plan a massive acquisition or aggressive expansion.

Macro Developments

I’ll begin once again with the weakness seen in the US, as that can have significant effects for Ambev as well. Although improving recently thanks to the latest economic data, the situation in the US advanced in an even more uncertain direction, with renewed tariff and geopolitical fears, a weakening dollar, Japan’s economic plans potentially hurting US treasuries, rising national debt leading to crisis worries, and several other factors that came in the brief period since the previous article was published - and this comes at the same time as US consumer confidence crashed to the lowest level since 2014.

All of these can put further pressure not only on the US consumer but also on the market’s expectations, as long-term rates are often a matter of much more than just what the Fed does, with factors such as the country’s growth prospects and risks being taken into account.

Although the situation in Ambev’s key markets isn’t that much better, as this weakness is ultimately a common thing in a high-rate environment, these factors in the US can lead to the market looking for ways to diversify, while the risk of the LATAM region could improve relative to the traditionally risk-free US, which is where Ambev can stand to benefit quite a bit as one of the biggest companies in the region.

However, another very important catalyst for Ambev and its consumers could come from the fact that Brazilian interest rates are expected to be cut significantly this year, with the market now expecting 50 basis point cuts at each of the next 7 policy meetings in 2026 after it was maintained at the latest meeting.

This is expected to bring the country’s Selic rate to 11.5% by the end of 2026 compared to 15% now, which can be an excellent catalyst for Ambev and Brazilian equities in general, especially if the market would be focused on the global diversification mentioned above, while it can also benefit its consumers and economy in general since this would be the opposite of rate hikes, which are meant to slow down the economy to fight inflation.

I’ll also mention once again that 2026 can turn out to be a great year for Ambev and the industry, as the management also expects. Alongside a holiday-rich calendar in Brazil this year, the FIFA World Cup will take place during the summer of 2026, which could be excellent for a significant boost in demand for beer/alcohol, with Ambev’s management even calling it “a great opportunity to connect again 2 of the greatest passions in Latin America, beer and soccer” back during their Q3 Earnings Call.

Regarding premiumization and other health-focused trends such as GLP-1, the company’s CEO highlighted the following during their Q4 Earnings Call, which

There are 2 different kinds of impact. One is attitudinal change. The other one is behavioral change. What happened last year was a change on the behavioral side due to the weather, mostly, okay? (...) And this is what explains the majority of the drop that we saw last year. (...) we are working with a very versatile category to attend more needs and trends. That's the reason why you see us developing zero alcohol beer. We are developing functional beers like gluten-free, lower calories. And we are also attending those consumers with more sweet flavor beers like Flying Fish that we introduced last year. So regarding the point about the GLP-1, we haven't observed any meaningful impact on our business. But like any other emerging trend, it requires time, more evidence, and as a consequence, I just want to say that we're going to keep monitoring and acting accordingly.

Ultimately, I believe it’s important to distinguish between the long-term premiumization trends and the weakness seen in recent years due to the environment. Yes, premiumization is a long-term trend that has been developing for decades as people switch to quality and specific niches over quantity, but the company has all the resources they need to not only adapt to these trends but even be the one that sets them as a global leader.

Risks Reiterated

Still, that doesn’t mean long-term premiumization trends won’t be a threat for the company’s volumes, and even though they can try to counter them with higher prices (which they already do, as premium/certain niches are sold above the average price index), that doesn’t guarantee a positive outcome for Ambev and its industry.

Meanwhile, broader macro pressure can continue alongside these long-term fundamental trends, potentially weakening Ambev’s ability to adapt due to lower budgets, which can have consequences on their results and the market’s expectations, while a disappointing 2026 despite the near-term tailwinds could be even worse for its valuation.

Valuation

For Ambev’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with their FCF of $3.80 billion in 2025 (not accounting for net borrowings because I want to capture their "true" organic potential), which I’ll continue to assume can grow at a CAGR of 4.5% over the first 5 years thanks to potential improvements in the environment, rate cuts, accretive M&A or investments (which is well below the market’s EPS and even revenue estimates), followed by a CAGR of 3.5% for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a discount rate of 11% for a solid margin of safety but also to account for their potential, with 15.67 billion shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $51.24 billion, for an intrinsic value of $3.27 per share, which is slightly above the current $3.11 despite using a relatively conservative margin of safety and growth expectations below the market’s estimates.

Conclusion

I reiterate ABEV’s Buy rating, as the company remains in a very solid financial position, is able to produce a lot of cash, and should stand to benefit from near- and long-term tailwinds as a market leader.

There are clearly risks coming from the region and broader environment, but improvements in these can also turn out to be strong opportunities, placing the overall risk-reward still in the company’s favor.

data.title: Biggest S&P 500 Winners Last 5, 10, 20 Years
data.description:
The S&P 500's annualized total return over the last five years has been roughly 13.5%, but the average stock currently in the index has seen an annualized total return about 2.6 percentage points less at just 10.9%.

Just over 20% of stocks in the S&P 500 have posted annualized returns of 20%+ over the last five years, but below is a list of the 15 companies with the strongest 5-year returns. Each of these stocks has gained more than 40% annually since early 2021!

While Nvidia (NVDA) is by far the biggest stock on the list of five-year winners and ranks second, Comfort Systems (FIX) - which installs HVAC and building systems - gets the gold with a 5-year annualized total return of 87.2%.

Super Micro (SMCI) gets the bronze at 56.9%, followed by Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and EMCOR (EME). Other notables on the list of 5-year winners include Broadcom (AVGO), Arista Networks (ANET), Quanta (PWR), Seagate (STX), and General Electric (GE). While GE struggled during the first 20 years of this century, it has lit a fire during the 2020s.

We also looked at returns over the last ten and twenty years to find stocks that have been the most consistent winners over lengthy time frames. As mentioned earlier, the S&P 500 has returned 13.5% annually over the last five years. Over the last ten years, the index has returned 15.6% annually, while it has gained 10.8% annually over the last twenty years.

In the S&P right now, there are only five stocks that have doubled the index annually over the last five, ten, and twenty years. These five stocks are shown below sorted by current market cap.

Three semiconductor stocks make up the list of five (NVDA, Lam Research (LRCX), and KLA (KLAC)), while the other two are the aforementioned Comfort Systems and an energy stock: Texas Pacific Land (TPL).

While there are only five S&P 500 stocks that have doubled the return of the index itself over the last five, ten, and twenty years, there are ten stocks that have posted 20%+ annualized returns over all three time frames.

These ten stocks are highlighted below, once again led by Nvidia, Lam Research, and KLA. Additional stocks that have yet to be mentioned are Amphenol (APH), Monolithic Power (MPWR), and Axon Enterprise (AXON).

So far we have only discussed winners, but we'll close with a list of S&P 500 stocks that have struggled mightily in the last two decades. In the index right now, there are nine stocks that have posted annualized total returns of less than 5% over each of the last five, ten, and twenty years.

Some of the more well-known names on the list include Zimmer (ZBH), Baxter (BAX), Conagra (CAG), and Campbell's (CPB).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wynn Resorts: Premium Positioning Powers A Multi-Year Growth Story
data.description:
Introduction

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) just reported its Q4 results for the year, and business results were mixed in that there were both positives and negatives. While revenues held steady and softer Vegas and Boston results were offset by momentum in Macau, I think investors are missing the bigger picture. Macau is now driving the largest share of revenues through strong VIP and premium growth, and Wynn's portfolio has some of the best luxury integrated resorts and casinos. In addition, the company has a strong balance sheet that’s allowed for significant capex investment to build out the Wynn Al Marjan Island tower in the UAE, which is on track for a spring 2027 opening and should start to contribute meaningfully to free cash flow.

It’s for those reasons the stock trades expensively on trailing numbers, but I think for investors who can look out a few years to potential EBITDA acceleration and free cash flow growth ahead, they’ll likely be well rewarded.

A Look Back at Q4 and FY'25 Results

Wynn Resorts wrapped up 2025 with a weaker-than-expected Q4. Revenues came in at $1.87 billion, up slightly compared to last year’s $1.84 billion and in line with consensus, but EPS of $1.17 missed by 31 cents and was down compared to last year.

When looking at what drove results this year, Wynn's Las Vegas operations generated $688 million of revenue, 1.6% lower compared to last year. While it continues to be a market leader with share gains and had a table games win percentage that came in at the high end at 26%, it did have a slight dip in RevPAR (revenue per available room) from management deliberately prioritizing higher rates over occupancy.

At Boston Harbor, revenues for the quarter came in at $210.2 million, about 1.1% lower, with slot revenues up 2% with growth in table drop and RevPAR. At Macau, the company continues to invest capex through the recently opened Gourmet Pavilion, Wynn Macau room refresh, and Chairman's Club Expansion at Wynn Palace (tripling its size to nearly 100,000 square feet with premium gaming and bespoke amenities, approved just in time for Chinese New Year). All of these are efforts to capture high-value customers in premium segments across its growing global footprint. Revenues in Macau now make up the company’s largest share at $967.7 million, and growth is being delivered through volume momentum, with VIP turnover up 48%.

When looking at overall metrics and profitability for the year, total property EBITDA now sits at over $2.2 billion a year. Las Vegas achieved $240.8 million in EBITDA at a 35% margin and benefitted from an $8 million positive hold swing despite a 4.1% increase in daily operating expense to $4.6 million from payroll, repairs, and bad debt. Boston generated $57 million in EBITDA at a 27.1% margin, supported by cost controls that limited daily opex growth to under 1% despite labor pressures. Finally, Macau produced $270.9 million in EBITDA at a 28% margin. That was weighed down by a $16 million VIP hold shortfall and a mass hold roughly 250bps below last year. The incremental costs from the full quarter Gourmet Pavilion and variable volume-related expenses also played a role.

As for the balance sheet, Wynn’s balance sheet is strong with total liquidity of $4.7 billion, most of that coming in the form of revolvers. Debt is structured at both the holdco and subsidiary levels. At the subsidiary level, the company’s Macau operations have two entities (Wynn Macau/Wynn Palace structures) that are currently carrying $5.79 billion of debt. At the holdco level, Wynn Resorts Finance (WRF), the company, carries $3.28 billion of debt. There’s also $877 million of debt against Las Vegas and another $598 million held in a JV.

While this structure may be confusing, Wynn does this to limit its exposure in the event of default and isolate its risk. With leverage at 4.4x company-wide (weighted average against all entities), the company has strong free cash flow generation that supports capex spending for ongoing projects. This quarter, the company spent $171 million on capex and another $26 million in dividends ($0.25 multiplied by 103 million shares outstanding).

Several Levers for EPS Uplift

Looking ahead, I think Wynn is well-positioned this year, given signs in Q4 that things are improving. Las Vegas is seeing casino volumes and RevPAR hold up well, Boston remains solid aside from weather-related dips, and Macau posted January volumes slightly above Q4 levels. This month, with Chinese New Year, Wynn should start to see an additional lift with the expanded Chairman's Club at Wynn Palace now approved and open, which management is describing as something that “will delight [their] best customers.” So while no Q1 guidance was provided, I do think these tailwinds set up the company well for next quarter.

But for the rest of 2026, I’d expect another good year. One of the reasons for this is that Macau’s GGR recovery has re-accelerated, with multiple houses now looking for high single- to double‑digit growth, driven disproportionately by premium mass. That directly benefits Wynn given its outsized exposure to premium customers and recent commentary about deliberately investing in VIP hosting and player development. As well, management highlighted that 2026 drivers are intact despite renovation headwinds, with group booking pace in Las Vegas “doing really, really well,” which lets them yield transient and casino segments harder on rate and mix. That aligns with my view that group/convention strength will offset the Encore Tower disruption via higher ADRs rather than chasing occupancy.

To put it in perspective, if Macau continues to grow in high single digits with premium outpacing base mass, I think Wynn can drive EBITDA via mix and rate even if China macro is only mediocre while Vegas fills the gap with higher‑rated group business that makes up for fewer Encore room nights.

As we’ve already dug into, Wynn is coming into 2026 with substantial liquidity versus its capex plan and no existential refinancing overhang, which gives them room to keep investing in guest products (Macau premium, Vegas rooms, Al Marjan) without sacrificing resilience. That balance sheet context makes it easier to underwrite a good year in 2026. With $750-850 million in capex for the development of Al Marjan Island, the company should start to see payback on this investment once it’s up and running next year.

Beyond 2026, I think there’s a multi-year story here with Al Marjan. Right now, it’s on track for a spring 2027 opening as a large‑scale integrated resort in the UAE, with the tower topped out around 70 floors and significant structural work already complete. The property will bring over 1,200 rooms plus nearly 300 high‑end suites, a large F&B and entertainment footprint, and the first true integrated resort experience in that market. Combined with premium‑heavy footprints in Las Vegas and Macau, I think that’ll give the company a portfolio leveraged to upper‑income consumers across the U.S., China, and the Gulf, all tied into wealth creation and outbound travel trends rather than just one macro cycle. On that point, it’s clear to me that management is long-term focused, and on the call they think about that a decade out, asking, “Where will we be in 2030 and 2032, and what will our business look like?” noting that they “feel very good about it” over the long haul.

Risks to Consider

With several casino properties that are tied to global gaming trends, it’s no secret that when times are tough, Wynn would get hurt in a downturn. If wealthy tourists stopped traveling and spending at Wynn properties, that would have a negative impact on the business. In a situation where revenues fall off and EBITDA gets cut in half, leverage would spike upwards. Mitigating this risk is the fact that a lot of the debt is held at the subsidiary level and the company enjoys a low cost of capital with no bonds trading with a spread more than 242bps above government debt. That indicates that the company should have little issue refinancing debt, at least the way things stand right now. Nevertheless, the business remains heavily exposed to Macau, where regulatory shifts, geopolitical tensions involving China, or renewed economic softness could materially impact premium and VIP volumes that have driven recent growth.

Second, the Wynn Al Marjan Island project in the UAE introduces a bit of risk in the sense that even with this being a greenfield development, it’s still a nascent gaming market, and delays beyond the Q1’27 opening or cost overruns could require a bigger equity commitment and delay future cash flows. Once this comes online, though, I do expect the company’s leverage to come down below 4x.

Wynn's Valuation is Justified Looking Out a Few Years

Wynn is rated favorably by the sellside with all ‘buy’ ratings and only 1 ‘hold’ rating. With an average price target of $142.79, analysts see about 24.5% upside from today’s levels, not including the company’s 0.88% dividend (source: Bloomberg). Speaking of the dividend, Wynn has a low payout ratio of 23%, paying 25 cents quarterly. After having cut its dividend in 2020 and then resuming it in 2023, there haven’t been any increases, but I’m okay with this given that the company is prioritizing safety and certainty of the dividend rather than chasing dividend growth, which we know can and has gotten it into trouble when cycles turn.

On valuation, Wynn looks optically expensive at 20.5x P/E and 9.2x EV/EBITDA. Both metrics are above peers, which trade at 16.6x P/E and 7.6x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). In my view, Wynn deserves its premium to peers for a few reasons. For one, Wynn’s entire model is built around the very top of the market (premium mass and VIP) rather than broad mid‑market exposure, which drives higher average customer worth and win per table/slot than competitors. As an example, Wynn properties have generated more gaming wins with fewer tables and machines than peers like MGM Resorts (MGM), which indicates that the assets and database are structurally more productive.

Another reason is the fact that Wynn’s future growth rates justify its higher TTM valuation multiples. When looking at analyst estimates for FY’26 and beyond, the company is expected to generate $8.12 in EPS in 2030, nearly double the $4.41 in EPS it’s generated in the last four quarters. So for a business that’s continuing to grow its EPS at a double-digit rate through the end of the decade, this valuation is justified.

Takeaway

All in all, I see 2026 as a year when Wynn grows into its premium positioning and capital program by absorbing known renovation and macro noise while setting up 2027–2030 as a period of accelerating free cash flow and better geographic balance, with Macau premiums, Las Vegas group demand, and Al Marjan’s ramp all pulling in the same direction. To me, this is one of the more underfollowed names in the market.

I think there’s a bit of a tug of war happening where traditional REIT and hospitality investors look at the 2026 P/E and balk but aren’t focused enough on what the true earnings power of this business will be if we just look three to five years out. With a strong balance sheet and a pipeline of opportunities to invest in and diversify growth internationally, I’m long Wynn Resorts.
